root_cause: regex-svc had its vulnerable WAF regex rule enabled. The rule used a catastrophic-backtracking pattern, ^(a+)+$, through a backtracking regex engine. The load container continuously sent malicious /check payloads, saturating the fixed worker pool and the container's 1 CPU limit, so health checks queued behind slow regex evaluations and timed out even though the process was still running.
actions_taken: Inspected container status, logs, live docker stats, compose configuration, and application source. Confirmed sreft-regex-svc was CPU-saturated while peer services were not, and verified the built-in mitigation WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1. Updated only regex-svc in docker-compose.yaml to set WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1, recreated only that service with docker compose up -d regex-svc, then verified the new environment, logs, /healthz responses, /check behavior, and CPU usage under continuing load.
postmortem: A request-triggered regex denial of service was allowed to affect the shared worker pool used by health checks. This made the service appear alive at the process level but unavailable at the request level. The immediate mitigation disabled the vulnerable WAF rule without touching unrelated services. Longer term, the rule should be replaced with a linear-time regex engine or bounded timeout, health checks should avoid contended request workers, and load tests should include adversarial inputs to catch CPU starvation before production.
